---
title: "PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage"
chapter_id: "planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "planforge"
  - "planforge_compiler_arch"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "tokenmana"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `coherence_exchange`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`, `moecot`; raw cache: `planforge`, `planforge_compiler_arch`, `cognitive_compilation`, `tokenmana`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange`, `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Large tasks require decomposition, scheduling, tiered model selection, and cost-aware routing.

The previous chapter defined the plan graph as the control artifact. This chapter specializes that graph into PlanForge-style DAG planning: decomposition, dependency inference, scheduling, model/tool tier assignment, cost accounting, quality predicates, escalation, and residual handling. PlanForge and the compiler-architecture variant both point to the same design pressure: orchestration should be compiled, not chatted through serially.

This chapter is also the book's first major efficiency mechanism after the Efficient ASI thesis. Intelligence arbitrage is not "use cheaper models whenever possible." It is "assign each node the minimum adequate capability plus the verification burden needed to know that adequacy was enough."

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Uniformly applying the strongest model to every task wastes compute and can obscure which tasks require verification or escalation.

Uniform routing hides structure. Some nodes are clerical, some require domain expertise, some require external tools, some require human approval, and some require adversarial review. Sending everything to the largest model wastes resources and can make the system less inspectable because no one knows which step was actually hard.

The opposite failure is under-routing: a weak worker handles a node whose quality predicate demands stronger reasoning, broader context, or independent verification. TokenMana adds the resource-governance frame: budgets are not just token counts; they include latency, review capacity, burst limits, and human cognitive cost.

## Core Claim

[planforge-dags-and-intelligence-arbitrage.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] PlanForge-style DAG planning can support intelligence arbitrage by assigning each node the minimum adequate capability and verification burden.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - PlanForge, PlanForge: A Compiler Architecture for AI Task Orchestration, The Coherence Exchange, Cognitive Compilation, and TokenMana - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a PlanForge DAG.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Accepted plan graph"] --> B["Primitive nodes"]
  B --> C["Dependency DAG"]
  C --> D["Capability tier annotation"]
  D --> E["Budget + risk annotation"]
  E --> F["Quality predicate"]
  F --> G["Worker / route assignment"]
  G --> H{"Predicate passes?"}
  H -- "yes" --> I["Merge artifact"]
  H -- "no" --> J["Escalate / residual"]
  I --> K["Cost-quality ledger"]
  J --> K
```

- Decompose goals into DAGs.
- Annotate nodes with capability tier, budget, context request, risk, and verification requirement.
- Merge redundant branches and escalate only when predicates fail.

The DAG should expose parallelism, critical path, redundant branches, cached work, and local repair opportunities. A failed node should not force global regeneration unless its dependencies require it. A passed node should not escape verification simply because it was assigned to a cheaper tier.

Cost accounting is evidence discipline, not marketing. A DAG may estimate tokens, time, tool cost, review cost, or latency, but any savings claim remains unpromoted until a reproducible run exists.

## Interfaces

The interface is the PlanForge DAG.

- Routing consumes capability annotations.
- VCM consumes context requests.
- Evidence records cost/quality results.

Minimum fields:

- `dag_id`
- `parent_plan`
- `nodes`
- `edges`
- `capability_tiers`
- `context_requests`
- `quality_predicates`
- `verification_requirements`
- `budget_limits`
- `route_assignments`
- `escalation_policy`
- `residuals`
- `cost_quality_ledger`

Routing consumes capability annotations. VCM consumes context requests. Evidence records cost, quality, residuals, and verification. Cognitive Compilation can later lower DAG nodes into semantic atoms or target-specific jobs.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Dependency order is respected.
- Cost savings do not remove required verification.
- Escalation paths are explicit.

The efficiency invariant is bounded by verification: cost savings do not count if they delete required checks. A low-tier route can be correct only if the node's quality predicate and escalation policy remain intact.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Wrong tier selection.
- Dependency cycles.
- Economic optimization overriding safety.

Wrong tier selection produces cheap wrong work. Dependency cycles make a graph appear planned while it cannot dispatch. Economic optimization overriding safety is the Goodhart version of planning: the scheduler optimizes cost while silently erasing quality or authority requirements. Each failure should emit a residual, not a successful schedule.

## Minimal implementation

A DAG schema with node-level capability and verification annotations.

The smallest useful implementation is a `planforge_dag` schema and fixture. It should validate node and edge presence, capability tiers, quality predicates, verification requirements, budget limits, route assignments, escalation policy, residuals, and a cost-quality ledger. It does not prove acyclicity; that remains a planned test/proof target.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| DAG acyclicity test | Check that all dispatchable graphs are acyclic and dependencies precede dependents. | planned; not run |
| Capability tier assignment test | Check that each node has a minimum adequate tier, route assignment, and verification burden. | planned; not run |
| Escalation trigger test | Check that failed quality predicates escalate or emit residuals instead of being treated as complete. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:planforge.dag.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` | A dispatchable plan graph is acyclic and all dependencies precede dependents. | planned |
| `lean:planforge.dag.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.PlanForge` | A node whose quality predicate fails must escalate or emit a residual. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `planforge` | PlanForge | planning_control | Planning substrate. Goal-to-execution compilation, hierarchical decomposition, DAG planning, scheduling, intelligence arbitrage. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `planforge_compiler_arch` | PlanForge: A Compiler Architecture for AI Task Orchestration | planning_control | Later/alternate PlanForge framing. Prefer highest-quality/latest content after comparison. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `tokenmana` | TokenMana | resource_economics | Regenerative capacity mechanisms for load-stable token pricing. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage turns planning into scheduled, typed work. The system decomposes goals, annotates nodes, routes by minimum adequate capability, verifies quality predicates, and records cost-quality outcomes.

The chapter's efficiency claim stays conservative: intelligence arbitrage is a design principle until reproducible scheduler experiments show cost and quality results. The architecture still benefits now because every node must name its tier, verification burden, budget, escalation path, and residuals.
